method3.generated_article:
==Geography and ecology==
Jarvis Island is an uninhabited coral island in the central equatorial Pacific Ocean, about 40 km (25 mi) south of the equator at 0°22′S 160°01′W.[1] It is part of the central Line Islands, also known as the Teraina Islands, and has an emergent land area described as about 4.5 km² in NOAA reporting and 1,273 acres in U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service refuge planning documents.[1][2] The island is the crest of an ancient coral reef cap over a massive extinct volcano, with a dried former lagoon at its center where deep guano deposits accumulated.[1] Jarvis is low-lying and mostly sandy, reaching only about 7 m (23 ft) above sea level at its highest point.[1]

The island has a tropical desert climate, with high daytime temperatures, steady easterly trade winds, intense equatorial sunlight, scant rainfall of less than 1 m (40 in) per year, and no known natural freshwater lens.[1] Its terrestrial vegetation is sparse and consists mainly of bunch grass, vines, and low-growing shrubs, with no plants larger than shrubs.[1] These plant communities provide nesting, roosting, and sheltering habitat for seabirds, shorebirds, and numerous land hermit crabs.[1] Jarvis supports more nesting seabirds than any other island in the U.S. tropical Pacific Islands, and Jarvis Island alone has been described as home to an estimated 1 million sooty terns.[1][3]

The shallow marine habitats around Jarvis include a fringing reef, shallow back reefs, a steep forereef, and a moderate-sized reef terrace on the eastern windward side of the island.[1] The surrounding marine ecosystem is unusually productive because of wind-driven equatorial upwelling and topographic upwelling associated with currents around the island.[1] U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service planning documents describe Jarvis as one of the few places where terrestrial and marine tropical island ecosystems remain relatively intact and comparatively free from human impact.[2] The island and its surrounding waters have been identified as a living laboratory for studying island, coral reef, and deep marine habitats, including long-term climate change and El Niño–Southern Oscillation effects.[2]

The Jarvis Island National Wildlife Refuge includes the island and the surrounding territorial sea out to the three-mile limit.[2] The refuge encompasses 1,273 acres of emergent land and 36,214 acres of submerged lands and waters, for a total of 37,487 acres.[2] Refuge management has emphasized preservation of the complete terrestrial and marine ecosystem, with special attention to the island’s large seabird nesting colonies.[2] The fringing reef has been characterized as a healthy coral community, attributed in part to Jarvis’s remoteness and limited anthropogenic disturbance.[2]

Human-introduced invasive mammals affected the island’s bird life, and recovery following eradication has been noted for several seabird groups.[3][4] Educational and management materials report increases in nearly every bird species after eradication efforts on Baker, Howland, and Jarvis, with both shearwater species returning to Jarvis.[3] The ecological significance of Jarvis is also tied to its role within the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument, whose small emergent islands provide nesting habitat for millions of seabirds.[3]

==History==
The first known European sighting of Jarvis Island occurred on 21 August 1821, when Captain Brown of the British ship *Eliza Francis* encountered the island; it was named for the ship’s owners, Edward, Thomas and William Jarvis.[1][2] American whalers and other seafarers later noted the island’s guano deposits, and the United States Exploring Expedition surveyed Jarvis in 1840.[1] In 1857, Benson and Charles Judd, aboard the Hawaiian schooner *Liholiho*, took possession of the island for the American Guano Company under the Guano Islands Act of 1856.[1] Later that year, Commander Charles Henry Davis of the USS *St. Mary* surveyed Jarvis and formally claimed it for the United States.[4][5]

Guano was mined on Jarvis from 1857 until 1879, when the island was abandoned after many tonnes had been removed.[2][4][5] The United Kingdom annexed Jarvis in 1889, but did not develop renewed mining operations there.[2][4][5] In 1906, the island was leased to the Pacific Phosphate Company of London, although mining again was not developed.[4][5] The United States reoccupied and reclaimed Jarvis in 1935, and it was placed under the jurisdiction of the Department of the Interior by Executive Order 7368 on 13 May 1936.[3][5]

During World War II, Japanese attacks led to the evacuation of the American colonists in 1942, after which the island remained mostly uninhabited.[3][5] In 1957–1958, scientists occupied the settlement site known as Millersville during the International Geophysical Year.[5] Administrative responsibility passed in June 1974 to the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service within the Department of the Interior.[5]

==Transportation==
Jarvis Island has no regular transportation links, and marine vessels capable of extended open-ocean travel are the only practical means of reaching the island.[1] The island lies about 1,263 nautical miles from the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service management staff in Honolulu, and a voyage to Jarvis typically takes about eight days by ship.[1] Transportation is a major operational limitation for refuge management because of the island’s remoteness, lack of affordable and reliable vessel access, lack of field infrastructure, harsh environmental conditions, and safety concerns associated with landing.[1] In recent years, access has been provided by NOAA, the U.S. Coast Guard, and private charter vessels, often in connection with scientific or management expeditions to the central Pacific island refuges.[1] NOAA scientific cruises commonly visit Jarvis as part of broader expeditions to multiple Pacific Remote Island areas, rather than as dedicated trips to the island alone.[1] Public use and access are generally closed, with visits limited to authorized management, research, and other permitted activities.[1][2] Supplies brought ashore for authorized expeditions, including fuel, must be transported and stored under strict containment and removal requirements because of the island’s sensitive refuge status and lack of permanent infrastructure.[2]

==Military==
Jarvis Island's principal pre-war strategic role was as part of the American Equatorial Islands Colonization Project, through which the United States Department of Commerce placed U.S. civilians on Jarvis, Howland, and Baker to support proposed weather stations and landing fields for military and commercial aviation routes between Australia and California.[3] The scheme also served a territorial and strategic purpose, as U.S. officials sought to reinforce American claims to the remote equatorial islands as a check on Japan's eastward expansion in the Pacific.[3] The colonists, known as Hui Panalāʻau, were chiefly young Native Hawaiian men and other male students recruited in Hawaii, making the Jarvis occupation civilian in form but tied to U.S. defense and aviation planning.[3] Physical traces of these activities include remnants of short channels blasted through shallow fringing reefs in the west and east-terrace areas to improve small-boat access during mining and military use.[2] Refuge planning documents note that debris or other hazards may still wash ashore or remain on the island from earlier human habitation during the guano-mining and military eras.[4]

==National Wildlife Refuge==
Jarvis Island National Wildlife Refuge was established in 1974; at creation, it comprised the island and the surrounding waters, submerged lands, and emergent lands from the mean low-tide line to 3 nautical miles offshore.[5] The refuge is reported by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service as covering 429,853 acres, including 1,273 terrestrial acres and 428,580 submerged acres, while the World Database on Protected Areas lists a reported area of 1,744.87 km² and classifies it as a marine and coastal protected area.[1][2] When the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument was established in 2009, the refuge’s marine boundary was extended to 12 nautical miles, placing it within the monument’s broader protected management framework.[5]

Management of the refuge is guided by the National Wildlife Refuge System mission to conserve, manage, and, where appropriate, restore fish, wildlife, plant resources, and their habitats for present and future generations.[1] At Jarvis, the Fish and Wildlife Service describes the refuge’s core purpose as protecting and preserving the natural character of its fish, wildlife, plants, coral reef communities, tidal lands, submerged lands, waters, and associated resources.[1] Proposed uses of the refuge are reviewed through compatibility determinations, including a draft determination that found amateur radio operations would not detract from the refuge’s conservation mission if conducted under specified guidelines.[1] Scientific management has included periodic field assessments; in August 2024, a U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service Pacific monuments biology team visited Jarvis, the agency’s first trip to the refuge in more than six years.[3] The refuge is also included in NOAA coral reef monitoring work for the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument, including a report compiling Pacific Reef Assessment and Monitoring Program survey data from 2000 to 2017.[5]
